chunk_summary: This section outlines glycemic targets and annual complication screening protocols for youth-onset type 2 diabetes, emphasizing early intervention and tailored monitoring strategies to mitigate multi-organ risks.
learning_objectives: Understand glycemic targets (<6.5% or <7%) for youth-onset type 2 diabetes and their contextual application.
Learn the annual screening schedule for complications, including nephropathy, retinopathy, and liver disease.
Recognize the importance of self-monitoring and CGM in managing youth-onset type 2 diabetes.
Identify specific screening needs for pubertal females, such as PCOS features.
key_facts: HbA1c target: <6.5% when achievable without hypoglycemia; <7% early in therapy or with high psychosocial burden.
Screening schedule: Lipids, nephropathy, liver disease, BP, OSA, and depression annually starting at diagnosis.
Nephropathy screening: Urine albumin/creatinine ratio confirmed with two of three first-morning samples if elevated.
Retinopathy and neuropathy assessments: Annually from diagnosis, including vibration/light touch/ankle reflex tests.
Pubertal females: Annual PCOS screening for cycle irregularity, hirsutism, and acne linked to metabolic control.
reference_quote: "Measure HbA1c every 3 months until stable and at least twice yearly thereafter; a target <6.5% is appropriate for many youth when achievable without significant hypoglycemia." (ISPAD clinical practice consensus guidelines 2024)
document_key_topics: Glycemic targets in youth-onset type 2 diabetes
Self-monitoring and CGM in pediatric diabetes management
PCOS and metabolic risk in pubertal females
